text: The robotic XI platform is used to open the gastrocolic space, releasing the pancreatic duodenum block and mobilizing it medially using the back maneuver.

The surgeon carefully dissects the duodenum to access the retroperitoneum, identifying the inferior vena cava. The dissection continues cranially until the left renal vein is identified, marking the upper limit of dissection. The procedure then progresses with lateral dissection.

The surgeon uses robotic calipers to visualize the upper mesenteric artery and the aorta, demonstrating the mobility and precision of the robotic instruments. The robotic hook provides high-quality energy for dissection, while hemostasis control ensures a dry operating field with clear visibility of the anatomical structures.

The surgeon opens the posterior direction towards the spine, identifying the aorta artery and dissecting its anterior face. A wide lymphadenectomy is performed in the aortic intercave space, mobilizing and drying small lymph nodes, including retrocaval ones. An assistant uses a vacuum device to extract smoke from the surgical field.

The surgeon elevates the liver using an auxiliary clamp connected to the diaphragm and applies traction to the visceral biliary using another auxiliary clamp. A lymphadenectomy is performed on the hepatic islet, common hepatic artery, and surrounding lymph nodes, utilizing robotic tweezers for enhanced mobility and precision.
The surgical team uses a robotic hook to perform lymphadenectomy, taking advantage of its high degree of freedom for precise dissection and manipulation of lymph nodes.

The surgeon completes a 360-degree dissection of the hepatic artery, followed by lymphadenectomy of the pericoledocyan and periportal structures. The gastroduodenal artery is dissected and sealed with plastic clips. The hepatic artery, portal vein, and main biliary pathways are clearly identified. The dissection continues towards the hepatic duct, with the collar being pulled and mobilized, and all connective and periportal tissue dissected.

The surgeon temporarily occludes the hepatic duct with a bulldog clamp and performs a gastrotomy using laparoscopic linear staplers. The anterior surface of the pancreas is exposed, and both the lower and upper edges of the pancreas are released. The back angle of the pancreas is mobilized, followed by an enterotomy and careful mobilization of the duodenum. The fourth portion of the duodenum is mobilized until it meets the third portion. The pancreas is carefully mobilized to prepare for future anastomosis, ensuring good visibility of the entire pancreatic circumference.

The surgeon identifies the splenic vein in the retro-pancreatic position and performs dissection of the superior mesenteric vein. The superior mesenteric vein is clipped at its first regional branch, and the Whipple maneuver is executed using robotic dissection. The junction of the superior mesenteric vein with the splenic vein forming the portal vein is clearly visible. Pancreatotomy is performed using a harmonic scalpel until the pancreatic duct is identified, which is then sectioned with scissors. The remaining pancreatic tissue is sectioned with the harmonic scalpel. The view shows the pancreatic tissue, superior mesenteric vein, and splenic vein. The mobilization of the pancreatic tissue is carried out progressively with careful maneuvers until the entire pancreatic process is removed. The duodenum is tractioned to the right side of the patient, positioning the operating part on the right flank.

The surgeon carefully dissects the vasoconstrictors of the sinus process, followed by cramping for the final release of the operating part. The surgical field provides a general view of the dissection, including the left renal vein, aorta, hepatic artery, and superior mesenteric vein.

The surgeon removes the biliary vesicle and identifies the regional bone, initiating the reconstruction of the digestive tract. The pancreatic anastomosis is performed using a splint to identify the pancreatic duct, followed by a posterior continuous suture with prolene. The jejunal pancreatic anastomosis is completed using a robotic platform, providing excellent visualization and movement. The mucosal duct anastomosis is finalized with sutures between the pancreatic duct and the jejunal loop, including the mucosa.

The surgeon performs the mucosal duct anastomosis using separate PDS 5-0 sutures, followed by the introduction of a trans-anastomotic splint. The pancreatic-jejunal anastomosis is completed with polypropylene 3-0 sutures to ensure secure sealing between the pancreas and jejunum. The round ligament, previously dissected and released, is used to create a reinforcing patch around the pancreatic-jejunal anastomosis.

The surgeon begins the hepatic-jejunal anastomosis using a continuous suture technique with the invaginative structure of Schmieden. The anastomosis is completed by suturing the anterior wall after the posterior wall. A gastroenterostomy is performed 40 centimeters from the hepatic anastomosis using a mechanical linear stapler, followed by closing the gaps in two planes. An entero-enterostomy is also performed to minimize biliary reflux and reduce the possibility of afferent loop syndrome.

The surgeon carefully reviews hemostasis, placing reinforcement sutures at the anastomoses. A blade drain number twenty-four is positioned for drainage, and the surgical specimen is removed through an incision.
